From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Northern region of the European continent

Not to be confused with [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Satellite_image_of_Northern_Europe2.jpg/330px-Satellite_image_of_Northern_Europe2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Satellite_image_of_Northern_Europe2.jpg)

A composed satellite photograph of islands and continental areas in and surrounding the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") and [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea")

The northern region of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") has several definitions. A restrictive definition may describe **northern Europe** as being roughly north of the southern coast of the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea"), which is about [54°N](/wiki/54th_parallel_north "54th parallel north"), or may be based on other geographical factors such as [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate") and [ecology](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology").

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Europe_K%C3%B6ppen_Map.png/330px-Europe_K%C3%B6ppen_Map.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_K%C3%B6ppen_Map.png)

European climate. The [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map is presented by the [Climatic Research Unit](/wiki/Climatic_Research_Unit "Climatic Research Unit") of the [University of East Anglia](/wiki/University_of_East_Anglia "University of East Anglia") and the Global Precipitation Climatology Center of the [Deutscher Wetterdienst](/wiki/Deutscher_Wetterdienst "Deutscher Wetterdienst").

The climate is mainly [Oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (Cfb), [Humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (Dfb), [Subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") (Dfc and Dsc) and [Tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra") (ET).

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main articles: [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), [Scandinavian Peninsula](/wiki/Scandinavian_Peninsula "Scandinavian Peninsula"), [Fennoscandia](/wiki/Fennoscandia "Fennoscandia"), [Jutland](/wiki/Jutland "Jutland"), and [Baltic region](/wiki/Baltic_region "Baltic region")

Northern Europe might be defined roughly to include some or all of the following areas: [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), [Fennoscandia](/wiki/Fennoscandia "Fennoscandia"), the peninsula of [Jutland](/wiki/Jutland "Jutland"), the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_region "Baltic region") plain that lies to the east, and the many islands that lie offshore from mainland northern Europe and the main European continent. In some cases, [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") is also included, although it is only politically European, comprising part of the [Kingdom of Denmark](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Denmark "Kingdom of Denmark"), and not considered to be geographically in Europe.

The area is partly mountainous, including the northern volcanic islands of [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") and [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen"), and the mountainous western seaboard, [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), and also often includes part of the large plain east of the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea").

The entire region's climate is at least mildly affected by the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream"). From the west climates vary from [maritime](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") and maritime subarctic climates. In the north and central climates are generally [subarctic](/wiki/Subarctic "Subarctic") or [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") and to the east climates are mostly [subarctic](/wiki/Subarctic "Subarctic") and [temperate](/wiki/Temperate "Temperate")/[continental](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate").

Just as both climate and relief are variable across the region, so too is vegetation, with sparse [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra") in the north and high mountains, [boreal forest](/wiki/Boreal_forest "Boreal forest") on the north-eastern and central regions [temperate coniferous forests](/wiki/Temperate_coniferous_forests "Temperate coniferous forests") (formerly of which a majority was in the [Scottish Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") and south west [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway")) and [temperate broadleaf forests](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_forests "Temperate broadleaf forests") growing in the south, west and temperate east.

## Classifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Classifications")]

There are various definitions of northern Europe which always include the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"), often the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") and [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"), and sometimes [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: UN geoscheme classification")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Europe_subregion_map_UN_geoscheme.svg/330px-Europe_subregion_map_UN_geoscheme.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_subregion_map_UN_geoscheme.svg)

[Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe")

The United Nations geoscheme is a system devised by the [United Nations Statistics Division](/wiki/United_Nations_Statistics_Division "United Nations Statistics Division") (UNSD) which divides the countries of the world into [regional](/wiki/Region "Region") and [subregional](/wiki/Subregion "Subregion") groups, based on the [M49 coding classification](/wiki/UN_M49 "UN M49"). The partition is for statistical convenience and does not imply any assumption regarding political or other affiliation of countries or territories.[[1]](#cite_note-m49-1)

In the UN geoscheme, the following countries are classified as being in northern Europe:[[1]](#cite_note-m49-1)

as well as the dependent areas:

  + [Bailiwick of Guernsey](/wiki/Bailiwick_of_Guernsey "Bailiwick of Guernsey")
  + [Bailiwick of Jersey](/wiki/Bailiwick_of_Jersey "Bailiwick of Jersey")

### World Geographical Scheme for Recording Plant Distributions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: World Geographical Scheme for Recording Plant Distributions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/WGSRPD_Northern_Europe.svg/330px-WGSRPD_Northern_Europe.svg.png)](/wiki/File:WGSRPD_Northern_Europe.svg)

Northern Europe, as defined by the World Geographical Scheme for Recording Plant Distributions

The [World Geographical Scheme for Recording Plant Distributions](/wiki/World_Geographical_Scheme_for_Recording_Plant_Distributions "World Geographical Scheme for Recording Plant Distributions") is a biogeographical system developed by the international [Biodiversity Information Standards (TDWG)](/wiki/Biodiversity_Information_Standards_(TDWG) "Biodiversity Information Standards (TDWG)") organization, formerly the International Working Group on Taxonomic Databases. The WGSRPD standards, like other standards for data fields in botanical databases, were developed to promote "the wider and more effective dissemination of information about the world's heritage of biological organisms for the benefit of the world at large". The system provides clear definitions and codes for recording [plant](/wiki/Plant "Plant") distributions at four scales or levels, from "botanical continents" down to parts of large countries. The following countries are included in their classification of "northern Europe":[[4]](#cite_note-4)

* [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark")
* [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")
* [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland")
* [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland")
* [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway")
* [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden")
* [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")

as well as the dependent areas:

* [Åland](/wiki/%C3%85land "Åland")
* [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands")
* [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man")
* [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen")
* [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard")

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/AtlEurRelig.jpg/250px-AtlEurRelig.jpg)](/wiki/File:AtlEurRelig.jpg)

Map of Europe showing the largest religions by region. Islam is represented in green, Eastern Orthodox Christianity in blue, Roman Catholicism in purple, and the other colors represent branches of Protestantism.

Countries in northern Europe generally have developed economies and some of the highest standards of living in the world. They often score highly on surveys measuring quality of life, such as the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"). Aside from the United Kingdom, they generally have a small population relative to their size, most of whom live in cities. The quality of education in much of Northern Europe is rated highly in international rankings, with [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") and [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") topping the list among the [OECD](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") countries in Europe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Language")]

[Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") are widely spoken in northern Europe with [North Germanic languages](/wiki/North_Germanic_languages "North Germanic languages") being the most common first language in the Faroe Islands ([Faroese](/wiki/Faroese_language "Faroese language")),[[5]](#cite_note-5) Iceland ([Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language")),[[6]](#cite_note-6) Denmark ([Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language")),[[7]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-7) Norway ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"))[[8]](#cite_note-Vikor-8) and Sweden ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language")).[[9]](#cite_note-9) The [West Germanic language](/wiki/West_Germanic_language "West Germanic language") [English](/wiki/English_(language) "English (language)") is the most common first language in Jersey, Guernsey, the Isle of Man, the United Kingdom and Ireland, however, the West Germanic language [Scots](/wiki/Scots_(language) "Scots (language)") is also spoken as a minority language in parts of Scotland and Ireland.[[10]](#cite_note-Facts_about_Jersey-10)[[11]](#cite_note-English_in_UK-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Ranelagh-12)[[13]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Languages-13) Beyond this, the [Finnic languages](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") of [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") and [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language") are the most common first languages of Finland[[14]](#cite_note-14) and Estonia[[15]](#cite_note-15) respectively. The [Baltic languages](/wiki/Baltic_languages "Baltic languages") of [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language") and [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language") are the most common first languages of Lithuania[[16]](#cite_note-16) and Latvia[[17]](#cite_note-17) respectively. A number of [Celtic languages](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") are spoken in the British Isles including the [Brythonic](/wiki/Brittonic_languages "Brittonic languages") [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") and the [Goidelic](/wiki/Goidelic "Goidelic") [Scots Gaelic](/wiki/Scots_Gaelic "Scots Gaelic") and [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"). The Celtic languages [Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_language "Cornish language") and [Manx](/wiki/Manx_language "Manx language") have been revived since becoming classed as extinct, being now spoken to a limited extent in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") and the Isle of Man respectively.[[13]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Languages-13) The [Norman languages](/wiki/Norman_language "Norman language") of [Jèrriais](/wiki/J%C3%A8rriais "Jèrriais") and [Guernésiais](/wiki/Guern%C3%A9siais "Guernésiais") are spoken in Jersey and Guernsey, though are listed as endangered due to the increasing prominence of English in the islands.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

While not the most common first languages in any country, [Sámi languages](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages") such as [North Sámi](/wiki/North_S%C3%A1mi "North Sámi"), [Lule Sámi](/wiki/Lule_S%C3%A1mi "Lule Sámi") and [South Sámi](/wiki/South_S%C3%A1mi "South Sámi") are spoken in the transnational region of [Sápmi](/wiki/S%C3%A1pmi "Sápmi") and are listed as endangered.[[13]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Languages-13)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Religion")]

See also: [§ Reformation outside Germany](/wiki/Reformation#Reformation_outside_Germany "Reformation")

During the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") expanded into northern Europe and spread [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") among the [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples").[[20]](#cite_note-20) Christianity reached the peoples of [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") and the [Baltic region](/wiki/Baltic_region "Baltic region") in later centuries. The [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") along with the influence of [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") spread northward from [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), leading to written [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"), [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"), [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"),[[22]](#cite_note-Mackenzie1992-22) [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"),[[23]](#cite_note-Watkins-23) [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"), [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"), [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"), [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") and [Sámi languages](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages"). The [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people") were the last peoples to be converted in the 18th century.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Regional cooperation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Europe&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Regional cooperation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/White_Sea_Canal_map.svg/250px-White_Sea_Canal_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:White_Sea_Canal_map.svg)

The Russian [White Sea–Baltic Canal](/wiki/White_Sea%E2%80%93Baltic_Canal "White Sea–Baltic Canal"), connecting eastern Baltic Europe to the Arctic

The [Hansa](/wiki/New_Hanseatic_League "New Hanseatic League") group in the European Union comprises most of the northern European states, plus the Netherlands.

The [melting of the Arctic](/wiki/Melting_of_the_Arctic "Melting of the Arctic") is likely to create new possibilities and issues involving collaboration in the region.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
